coredata.dc:title: Plant-derived Ca, N, S-Doped carbon dots for fast universal cell imaging and intracellular Congo red detection

coredata.dc:description: Congo red (CR) is a hazardous pigment, posing increasing dangerous to the environment and human health. However, the in-situ detection of CR in living cells has not been reported, as far as we know. Here, negatively-charged green-emitting Ca, N, S-doped carbon dots (Mis-mPD-CDs) were fabricated from plant and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) by facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Mis-mPD-CDs were capable of rapidly detecting CR on the basis of their fluorescence quenching by CR due to the inner filter effect. This CR detection based on Mis-mPD-CDs displayed a linear range of 0.2–1.2 μM and a low limit of detection (58 nM), and was not interfered by metal ions, important biological molecules, and other dyes, showing high sensitivity and selectivity. More interestingly, Mis-mPD-CDs can rapidly enter and label animal cells (A549, 4T1, and HUVEC), fungi (S. cerevisiae, C. albicans, and T. reesei), and bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus) for long term with high stability and appealing biocompatibility. Based on these compelling characteristics, we applied Mis-mPD-CDs for sensing and imaging CR in living cells (A549, C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus) and zebra fish. On the other hand, the quantitative detection of CR by Mis-mPD-CDs was realized in real samples like fish tissues and industrial wastewater. This is the first report on applying CDs for rapid CR detection in living cells and in vivo. Mis-mPD-CDs provides a novel efficient platform for probing intracellular CR, expanding the applications of CDs as biosensors for toxic dyes.

(B) UV–vis absorption spectrum, (C) zeta potential, and (D) fluorescence spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs dissolved in water. (E) FTIR spectrum, and (F) full XPS spectrum of dried Mis-mPD-CDs. The high-resolution peaks in the XPS spectrum including (G) C1s, (H) N1s, and (I) O1s were presented.

The detection of CR by Mis-mPD-CDs. (A) Fluorescence spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs treated with CR at varied concentrations. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 and the CR concentrations in the range of 0.2–1.2 μM. (C) The fluorescence response of CDs to CR at distinct equilibration times. (D) The impact of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs. (E) The fluorescence response of Mis-mPD-CDs toward important biological molecules and other dyes. (F) The UV–vis absorption spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs, CR, and Mis-mPD-CDs + CR. Meanwhile, the physically-adding spectrum of Mis-mPD-CDs spectrum and CR spectrum was presented, which was indicated as theoretical. (G) The UV-is absorption band of CR, and the excitation and emission spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs. (H) The fluorescence response of Mis-mPD-CDs toward CR at different temperatures.

Confocal images of (A) animal cells (A549, 4T1 and HUVEC), (B) fungal cells (S. cerevisiae, C. albicans, and T. reesei), and (C) bacterial cells (E. coli and S. aureus) incubated with Mis-mPD-CDs for different time points.

The effect of (A) UV irradiation, (B) NaCl, and (C) temperature on the PL intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs. Evaluation of the Mis-mPD-CDs biocompatibility. (D) Viabilities of Hep G2 and HP cells treated with Mis-mPD-CDs. (E) Hemolysis rates of the red blood cells (RBCs) with the treatment of Mis-mPD-CDs. RBCs in PBS were utilized as the negative control, while Triton X-100 were explored as the positive control. (F) The impact of Mis-mPD-CDs at different concentrations on the cell growth of C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Imaging and sensing of intracellular CR based on Mis-mPD-CDs. The A549 was treated with CR for 30 min, C. albicans for 1 h, E. coli and S. aureus for 4 h. (B) The corresponding cellular fluorescence intensity of all these samples were measured by flow cytometry analysis. (C) The corresponding statistics of the flow cytometric results in (B).
CLSM images of zebrafish treated with 0, 5, 15 or 20 μM CR for 4 h, and then incubated with Mis-mPD-CDs (100 μg/mL) for 1 h.
Schematic illustration of Mis-mPD-CD preparation and their application for universal cell imaging and intracellular CR detection.
CR detection in fish tissue and industrial wastewater based on the Mis-mPD- CDs.

head.ce:title: Plant-derived Ca, N, S-Doped carbon dots for fast universal cell imaging and intracellular Congo red detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Congo red (CR) is a hazardous pigment, posing increasing dangerous to the environment and human health. However, the in-situ detection of CR in living cells has not been reported, as far as we know. Here, negatively-charged green-emitting Ca, N, S-doped carbon dots (Mis-mPD-CDs) were fabricated from plant and m-phenylenediamine (mPD) by facile one-step hydrothermal carbonization. Mis-mPD-CDs were capable of rapidly detecting CR on the basis of their fluorescence quenching by CR due to the inner filter effect. This CR detection based on Mis-mPD-CDs displayed a linear range of 0.2–1.2 μM and a low limit of detection (58 nM), and was not interfered by metal ions, important biological molecules, and other dyes, showing high sensitivity and selectivity. More interestingly, Mis-mPD-CDs can rapidly enter and label animal cells (A549, 4T1, and HUVEC), fungi (S. cerevisiae, C. albicans, and T. reesei), and bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus) for long term with high stability and appealing biocompatibility. Based on these compelling characteristics, we applied Mis-mPD-CDs for sensing and imaging CR in living cells (A549, C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus) and zebra fish. On the other hand, the quantitative detection of CR by Mis-mPD-CDs was realized in real samples like fish tissues and industrial wastewater. This is the first report on applying CDs for rapid CR detection in living cells and in vivo. Mis-mPD-CDs provides a novel efficient platform for probing intracellular CR, expanding the applications of CDs as biosensors for toxic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon-derived fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained extensive attention in recent years [1] after they were for the first time found during the treatment of single-wall carbon nanotubes [2]. Due to their excellent advantages in luminescence, biocompatibility, stability, and low cost, CDs have been greatly explored for broad applications in fluorescent-nano probes [3] as chemosensors and biosensors [4–7], optoelectronic devices [8,9], photo-catalysis [1,10,11], and biomedicine [12]. Recently, the application of CDs in detection of environmental pollutants has attracted increasing attention, which majorly focuses on the detection of metal ions [13]. Only several studies have utilized CDs for the detection of dyes, another type of key pollutant in environments [14–17]. For example, Zhao et al. have prepared red-emitting CDs from 1,2,4,5- benzenetetramine tetrahydrochlorid, which have been exploited as a fluorescent compound for the fast assay of methylene blue [14]. Zulfajri et al. have applied N-doped CDs synthesized from the extract of Averrhoa carambola and L-arginine as a fluorescent probe for sensing methyl orange with great sensitivity and high specificity [15]. Hua et al. reported yellow-emitting Se, N-co-doped CDs derived from selenourea and o-phenylenediamine for sensitive fluorescence measurement of crystal violet based on inner filter effect [16]. Bogireddy et al. have fabricated N-doped oxidized CDs using citric acid and urea to measure different toxic organic pigments such as methyl orange, methylene blue, bromophenol blue, and rhodamine 6G [18]. Yet, the utilization of CDs for in situ detection of pigments in living cells has not been reported, to the best of our knowledge.

Congo Red (CR) is a dangerous azo dye derived from benzidine [19], showing high toxicity to the environment and humans. A large amount of CR-containing wastewater is produced owing to the utilization of pigments and dyes in industries like dyestuffs, plastics, paper, leather, textiles, cosmetics, food, and pharmaceuticals [20]. CR is recalcitrant to microbial degradation and highly stable in the environment [21]. Even worse, upon entering the human body, CR transforms into benzidine, a carcinogen, and mutagen [22–24]. Moreover, CR possesses many other negative effects on human health causing diarrhea, vomiting, nausea, and allergic problems [25–27]. However, the detection of CR has not received much attention as other azo dyes like methylene blue, methyl orange, bromophenol blue, and crystal violet [28].

Currently, the CR available detection methods include the traditional UV–visible spectrophotometry [29], the newly-minted spectrophotometric method [30], and electrochemical method [31]. The detection of CR by UV–visible spectrophotometry has low sensitivity and pre-concentration of CR in water samples by different strategies is preferred, which is time-consuming with huge volume sample required [29]. The spectrophotometric method was developed for CR detection, which was on the basis of the catalytic activity of silver nanoparticles toward the CR oxidation via potassium bromate using the hydrochloric acid solution [30]. Unfortunately, this method was interfered by foreign ions Cr (VI), Cu (II), and Fe (III). Even worse, hydrochloric acid used for sample preparation was a dangerous solvent. The electrochemical method [32] was established via recording the cyclic voltammograms during electrochemical degradation of CR via graphene oxide nanoparticles prepared on glassy carbon electrode (GCE) [31]. When using this method, interference was observed from other metal ions like barium chloride, ammonium sulfate, copper acetate, and potassium nitrate [31]. All these methods suffer from long operation time, low sensitivity, poor selectivity, high cost, and intensive labor. Moreover, these CR assay can not detect CR in living cells. Although yellow-green emissive CDs obtained by the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and p-phenylenediamine have been utilized for sensitive fluorescence measurement of CR in solutions [33], no CD-based method for CR detection in cells and in vivo have been reported, as far as we know. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop efficient strategies for intracellular CR detection.

Herein, Mis-mPD-CDs were prepared from plant Miswak and mPD by one-step hydrothermal carbonization, which were negatively-charged, Ca, N, S-doped, and green-emitting (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence of Mis-mPD-CDs was quenched in the addition of congo red (CR) owing to the inner filter effect. More importantly, Mis-mPD-CDs can rapidly image a broad range of cells for long-term including animal cells, fungi, and bacteria with great stability and neglectable cytotoxicity. Base on these fascinating properties, Mis-mPD-CDs were successfully employed for probing CR in living cells and zebra fish. The quantitative detection of CR in real samples including fish tissues and industrial wastewater were also achieved via Mis-mPD-CDs.

CDs were fabricated from the roots of an ancient plant Miswak (Salvadora persica) and mPD using the facile, bottom-up solvothermal approach. In brief, 100 mg Miswak powder and 30 mg mPD were dissolved in 30 mL water in a 50-mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, which was incubated for 12 h at 180 °C. Following by cooling down at room temperature, the solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 15,000 rpm to collect the supernatant that was further filtered through a filter membrane of 0.22 μm to get rid of large particles. The obtained CD sample was dialyzed with milliQ water using a dialysis bag of 1000 Da cutoff for 6 h, freeze-dried, and preserved at 4 °C for use in future.

The characterization of Mis-mPD-CDs were performed as described in our previous research, including the transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging, the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum, the zeta potential value, the fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum, the fluorescence spectra, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) spectra, and the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) calculation [34–37].

16.6 μL 3 mg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs were mixed with 100 μM CR of varied volumes to reach different final concentrations of CR (0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0, 1.2, 1.4, 1.7, 1.9, 2.1, 2.3, and 2.5 μM) by adjusting the final volume of each mixture to 1 mL using milliQ water. The fluorescence emission spectra of these mixed samples were taken under the excitation of 440 nm. To evaluate the detection selectivity of Mis-mPD-CDs for CR, the fluorescence of other pollutants (methylene blue, bromophenol blue, and crystal violet), metal ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ag+, and Cd2+) and biological molecules (human serum albumin, glycine, melamine, dopamine, and glutathione) were tested individually in a similar way to CR. The mapping of the working curve was carried out via log (F0 -F)/F
0 and the CR concentration, where F0 and F were respectively the fluorescence intensity of the Mis-mPD-CDs without and with CR.

The limit of detection (LOD) was determined based on the following equation [38]:
LOD = 3σ/K
Where K is the slope for the linearity range and σ is the standard deviation of the blank (n = 11).

The cancerous cells lung cancer cells (A549) and breast cancer cells (4T1), and normal cells human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were cultured separately in cDMEM. The cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. After overnight growth, the culture medium in each well was replaced with Mis-mPD-CDs (50 μg/mL) and incubated for different times (2, 10, 30, 40, and 60 min) at 37 °C. Then, the cellular imaging was examined using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 63 × oil immersion objective. The fluorescence emission was 500 nm–550 nm upon the excitation at 488 nm.

Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae) and Candida albicans (C. albicans) were grown in Yeast Extract Peptone Dextrose (YPD) medium (20 mg/mL peptone, 10 mg/mL yeast extract, and 20 mg/mL glucose) and Yeast Malt (YM) medium (5 g/l peptone, 10 g/l glucose, 3 g/l yeast extract, and 3 g/l malt extract), respectively, at 28 °C with 100 rpm. Trichoderma reesei (T. reesei) were grown in Sabouraud Dextrose Ager (SDB) at 28 °C with 200 rpm. Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were used as the Gram-negative and Gram-positive [39,40] representative bacteria, respectively. In fresh LB medium (5 mg/mL yeast extract, 10 mg/mL tryptone, and 10 mg/mL NaCl) these two types of bacteria were cultured and incubate at 37 °C with 200 rpm in a shaking incubator overnight. The microorganism cells were collected by centrifugation and treated with 50 μg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs in the corresponding culture medium. After incubation for different period of time (2, 10, 30, 40, and 60 min), the microorganism cells were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min for confocal imaging.

The CD solution was irradiated for varied times as displayed in the text using a 365-nm UV lamp with 20 mW. The PL intensities of the irradiated solution were measured via spectrofluorophotometer at 510 nm with λex = 440 nm. Likewise, the PL intensityty of the CD samples at different NaCl concentrations (0.0, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 M) and various temperatures (30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, and 100 °C) were recorded.

Hep G2 or HP cells were cultivated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 utilizing complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (cDMEM) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum. With a density of 5 × 103 cells per well, cells were added in a 96-well plate, followed by overnight incubation. Then, the culture media was discarded and Mis-mPD-CDs were added at different final concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, or 200 μg/mL in fresh culture media. After another 24-h incubation, the cell viability was assessed using standard MTT method.

A healthy mouse was utilized to collect fresh blood through an anticoagulation tube to prevent coagulation. Next, the serum in the mouse blood was discarded through centrifugation and washing to acquire the red blood cells (RBCs) [41]. Then RBCs resuspended in 0.9% NaCl solution were mixed with Mis-mPD-CDs at varied final concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, or 200 μg/mL. The samples were centrifugated at 2000 rpm for 5 min after being incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Then, the absorbance of the supernatants was assayed at 492 nm on a microplate reader. RBCs in 1% Triton X-100 solution and PBS were utilized as the positive control and negative control individually. The hemolysis percentage was measured utilizing the formula as below:
H e m o l y s i s % =

)

× 100 %

The cytotoxicity of Mis-mPD-CDs on C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus were assessed by recording the optical density value at 600 nm (OD600) via the microplate reader. Briefly, the overnight culture of C. albicans, E. coli, or S. aureus was diluted 30 times using the corresponding culture medium, and then were grown until OD600 reached 0.5. Afterward, the log phase microorganisms were inoculated 1:10 into 150 μL fresh culture medium containing varied concentrations of Mis-mPD-CDs in the 96-well plate and cultivated for 12 h at 37 °C. Then, OD600 of each well was measured using a Multiskan FC microplate photometer (Thermo).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CR in animal cells and microorganisms
ce:section.ce:para: A549 cells were cultivated at 37 °C under 5% CO2 utilizing complete Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (cDMEM) that contains 10% fetal bovine serum. With a density of 5 × 103 cells per well, cells were added in a 96-well plate, followed by overnight incubation. Then the cells were incubated with 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 μM CR for 30 min. Then the culture medium was replaced by 50 μg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs in cDMEM. After incubation for 30 min, the cells were imaged by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 63 × oil immersion objective. The fluorescence emission was 500 nm–550 nm upon the excitation at 488 nm. The detection of CR in microorganisms including C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus were also performed in the same way. Besides, the flow cytometry were employed to record the cellular fluorescence intensity in different samples using FITC channel with the excitation at 488 nm.

Zebrafish at 5 dpf (days post fertilization) were cultured at room temperature using standard zebrafish E3 culture medium (0.33 mM MgSO4.7H2O, 0.33 mM CaCl2, 0.17 mM KCl, and 5 mM NaCl) in 2 mL tubes. First, the zebrafish larva were incubated with 0, 5, 15 and 20 μM CR for 4 h before treating with 100 μg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs for 1 h. Then the confocal images of the zebra fishes were obtained on a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica SP8) with a 10 × objective. The fluorescence emission was 500 nm–550 nm upon the excitation at 488 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD-based CR detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Following reference [42], tissues sample collected from pomfret and opium were minced and freezed. 10 g of the thawed tissues were dissolved in 30 mL acetonitrile, followed by 1 h sonication. The supernatant was obtained by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 5 min, in which anhydrous sodium sulfate was dissolved. Acetonitrile was then evaporated by rotary evaporator, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was used to disperse the samples. For CR detection, 100 μM CR solution was diluted in milliQ water to have the final concentrations of CR as 0.0, 0.4, 0.8, and 1.2 μM, and then mixed with 16.66 μL of 3 mg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs. The volume of the sample was adjusted to 1 mL. Under the excitation of 440 nm, the fluorescence emission spectra of these mixtures were taken. The detection of CR in wastewater from industry using Mis-mPD-CDs were identical as described above.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism of CR on CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CR, and CDs + CR were measured. The fluorescence intensity of 50 μg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs at 510 nm with the excitation at 440 nm at 30, 45, 60, 75, and 90 °C were examined before and after the addition of 2.5 μM CR.

CDs were fabricated from an ancient plant Miswak roots and mPD using a facile, bottom-up solvothermal method, which was named as Mis-mPD-CDs. TEM image showed that Mis-mPD-CDs were in spherical form and possessed an average diameter of 19.6 nm (Fig. 1 A). They were uniform in size and well monodispersed, similar to other CDs prepared from plants [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the UV–vis spectrum of Mis-mPD-CDs exhibited two absorption bands at 288 nm and 420 nm. The
π −
π ∗
transition of the aromatic
sp 2
domains [45] might account for the 288 nm peak, while the
n −
π ∗
transition of Mis-mPD-CDs might cause the broad peak at 420 nm [46,47]. Mis-mPD-CDs was negatively-charged, displaying a charge of −6.66 mV as analyzed via zeta potential measurement (Fig. 1C). The fluorescence emission spectrum of Mis-mPD-CDs was dependent on wavelength. The emission intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs raised gradually when the excitation wavelength was increased from 320 to 440 nm, and then fell sharply from 460 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 1D). Furthermore, the red-shifting of the Mis-mPD-CDs emission peaks from 462 nm to 530 nm were observed in the excitation wavelength range of 320–500 nm (Fig. 1D). Collectively, Mis-mPD-CDs was green-emitting and possessed the maximal emission at 510 nm with the excitation at 440 nm. The PL quantum yield of 19% was recorded under the excitation of 440 nm for Mis-mPD-CDs in water against quinine sulfate as a reference [48], which is much higher than that of other plant-based CDs [49–53].

To investigate their composition, Mis-mPD-CDs were analyzed by FTIR [54], and XPS. As displayed in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1E), the broad absorption bands around 3175 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of N–H (ν(N–H)) and O–H (ν(O–H)) [55,56], respectively. The peaks located at 2928 cm−1, 2830 cm−1, and 2711 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–H (ν(C–H)). The peak at 1617 cm−1 can be due to the C=O stretching vibration (ν(C=O)), probably resulting from the –COOH group or the amide bond (–CONH–). The two peaks at 1404 cm−1 and 1365 cm−1 may stem from the C-N stretching vibration (ν(C-N)), the 1122 cm−1 one from the C–O–C stretching vibration (ν(C–O)), and the two at 878 cm−1 and 773 cm−1 from the stretching vibration of Ca. Moreover, the elemental components and surface groups of Mis-mPD-CDs were studied by XPS. Mis-mPD-CDs consisted of 70.5% carbon, 8.7% nitrogen, 13.2% oxygen, 6.3% calcium, and 1.4% sulfur elements. The full XPS spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs showed five peaks at 284.2 eV, 347.4 eV, 398.5 eV, 438.0 eV, and 530.1 eV for C1s, Ca2p, N1s, Ca2s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1F). The C1s band contained three distinctive peaks that were related to carbon sp2 (C=C, 284.0 eV), carbon sp3 (C–N, 285.0 eV), and carbon sp3 (C-O, 286.5 eV) (Fig. 1g). The N1s band was differentiated into three peaks at 398.4 eV for the pyridine N (C-N-C), 399.1 eV for the amino N (N-C), and 400.0 eV for the pyrrole N (C-NH2) (Fig. 1H). The two fitted peaks at 529.0 and 530.1 eV in the O1s spectrum can be owning to the oxygen element in C-O and C=O forms, respectively (Fig. 1I). The XPS data further evidenced that these Mis-mPD-CDs were successfully doped with Ca, N, and S elements, and functionalized with the amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Congo red (CR) using Mis-mPD-CDs through the inner filter effect
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs was decreased sharply in the presence of 1.0 μM CR, showing only 50% that of Mis-mPD-CDs alone (Fig. 2 A). When the concentration of CR was up-regulated from 0 to 2.5 μM, the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs gradually declined. In the range of 0.2–1.2 μM, we observed a great linear relationship between CR concentrations and
F
0 − F
F 0
and obtained the equation
F
0 − F
F 0
= 0.997 × CCR (μM) + 0.994, whose correlation coefficient R2 was 0.994 (Fig. 2B). On the basis of 3σ/slope, the limit of detection (LOD) of Mis-mPD-CDs for CR was determined to be 58 nM, which is lower than the reported LODs of the existing CR measurement methods such as spectrophotometric method (0.6 × 10−3 M) [30], and voltametric behavior of CR at GO-GCE (2.4 × 10−7 M) [31]. This demonstrates that the CR detection based on Mis-mPD-CDs has better sensitivity than the other CR assays. Moreover, the fluorescence of Mis-mPD-CDs was quenched by CR within 1 min (Fig. 2C), showing highly rapid fluorescence quenching response toward CR. Furthermore, the selectivity of Mis-mPD-CDs toward CR over metal ions (Fig. 2D), biomolecules, and other pollutants (Fig. 2E) were examined. Metal ions like Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ag+, and Cd2+ at 500 μM, which was 200-fold that of CR (2.5 μM), have no impact on the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs. Similarly, the key biological molecules such as human serum albumin (HSA), glycine (Gly), melamine (Mel), dopamine (DA), and glutathione (GSH) did not have any impact on the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs. Moreover, the other pollutants like methylene blue (MB), bromophenol blue (BPB), and crystal violet (CV) did not change the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs (Fig. 2E). These findings demonstrate Mis-mPD-CDs can be applied for fast CR detection with high specificity and great sensitivity.

The possible sensing mechanism of Mis-mPD-CDs for CR was explored. The applications of CDs for detecting environmental pollutants are generally based on their interactions with environmental pollutants [13]. These interactions either reduce the CD fluorescence intensity by quenching effect, or enhance fluorescence intensity by overturning the quenching effect. Obviously, the CR detection was established through the fluorescence quenching of Mis-mPD-CDs by CR. Therefore, the underlying mechanism of Mis-mPD-CDs fluorescence quenching by CR was investigated. The experimental UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Mis-mPD-CDs/CR mixture marched well with the theoretical one obtained by physically summing up the spectra of Mis-mPD-CDs and CR (Fig. 2F), revealing that the interaction between Mis-mPD-CDs and CR is too weak to form complexes and the electron transfer between Mis-mPD-CDs and CR was unfeasible. Thus, the quenching effect of Mis-mPD-CDs by CR was not due to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [42,57]. The absorption band of CR at 400–650 nm fully overlapped with the emission spectrum of Mis-mPD-CDs (Fig. 2G), implying that the observed quenching effect of Mis-mPD-CD may be ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE) or the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [58]. Moreover, the fluorescence response of Mis-mPD-CDs toward CR was not dependent on temperature (Fig. 2H), which further supports IFE. Collectively, IFE is probably the quenching mechanism for Mis-mPD-CDs by CR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of different animal, fungal and bacterial cells using Mis-mPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we assessed whether Mis-mPD-CDs can enter and image cells. Different animal cells were used, including cancerous cells (A549 and 4T1) and normal cells (HUVEC) (Fig. 3 A and Fig. S1). Mis-mPD-CDs permerlized into all the three cells very fast (within 2 min) and lighted them up, making them to display green fluorescence. The green fluorescence in the cells remained strong at 60 min, suggesting that Mis-mPD-CDs could stain in the cells for long term. Interestingly, Mis-mPD-CDs were majorly accumulated in nucleoli in all the three animal cells. Some CDs have been reported to stain nucleoli [59,60]. Furthermore, we investigated the staining ability of Mis-mPD-CDs on fungal cells, utilizing the model fungi including S. cerevisiae, C. albicans, and T. reesei (Fig. 3B and Fig. S2). All the three fungi could be well stained by Mis-mPD-CDs. Mis-mPD-CDs can enter the fungal cells within 2 min. Strong green fluorescence was observed in all the fungal cells with the treatment of Mis-mPD-CDs for 2–60 min. Given that animal cells and fungal cells are both eukaryotic cells, we'd like to see if Mis-mPD-CDs can label prokaryotic cells. Therefore, E. coli and S. aureus, the well-known Gram-positive and Gram-negative model bacterium respectively, were treated with Mis-mPD-CDs for varied times (Fig. 3C and Fig. S3). Similar to animal cells, high green fluorescence was found in both E. coli and S. aureus within 2 min, which was maintained for 60 min, suggesting that Mis-mPD-CDs can also rapidly label the bacterial cells for long time. It is worthy mentioning that all the confocal imagings were taken without sample washing. Although the fluorescent CDs have been well demonstrated to be a type of powerful nanomaterial for cell imaging, the currently available CDs generally stain specific type of cells [61–66]. One CD for all cells is rarely reported. Therefore, it is of great importance to find that Mis-mPD-CDs can stain a wide range of cells including both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. All together, these results demonstrate that Mis-mPD-CDs possess excellent capability in rapid wash-free long-term cellular imaging for animal cells, fungi, and bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mis-mPD-CDs displayed high stability and excellent biocompability
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability of Mis-mPD-CDs that is key for practical applications were evaluated under different experimental conditions including UV irradiation time, ionic concentration, and temperature (Fig. 4 A–C). No change was observed in the PL of Mis-mPD-CDs irradiated at 365 nm for 90 min (Fig. 4A). Meanwhile, the PL of Mis-mPD-CDs stayed unchanged in water with different NaCl concentrations (Fig. 4B). Moreover, the PL of Mis-mPD-CDs stayed constant with the elevated temperature, even up to 100 °C (Fig. 4C). All these findings demonstrate that Mis-mPD-CDs possess an excellent PL stability, which would benefit their application in CR detection.

Furthermore, to analyze the safety of Mis-mPD-CDs, the relative viabilities of Hep G2 and HP cell in the presence of Mis-mPD-CDs were evaluated by MTT assay (Fig. 4D). More than 84% of both Hep G2 and HP cell were alive with the treatment of Mis-mPD-CDs from 0 to 200 μg/mL, implying that Mis-mPD-CDs is nontoxic within the concentrations (50 μg/mL) that are utilized for CR detection. Furthermore, the hemolysis assay showed that the hemolytic activity of Mis-mPD-CDs was negligible against the red blood cells (RBCs) even though their concentration was raised up to 200 μg/mL (Fig. 4E), implying the excellent hemocompatibility of Mis-mPD-CDs. Additionally, the cell viability of C. albicans, E. coli, or S. aureus treated with up to 100 μg/mL Mis-mPD-CDs was not changed obviously in comparison to the untreated cells (Fig. 4F). Taken together, Mis-mPD-CDs exhibit high PL stability and excellent biosafety, making them suitable for CR detection in industry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Mis-mPD-CDs for intracellular CR detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering their outstanding cell penetration ability, high stability, and great biocompability, we applied Mis-mPD-CDs to detect intracellular CR in animal cells, fungi and bacteria (Fig. 5 ). As shown by CLSM, the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs in A549 cells was reduced significantly as the CR concentration was increased from 0 to 20 μM (Fig. 5A). At 20 μM CR, the green fluorescence is hardly visible. When using Mis-mPD-CDs to probe CR in fungus C. albicans, the similar result to animal cells was obtained (Fig. 5A). Mis-mPD-CDs can also detect CR in C. albicans by the green fluorescence reduction caused by the presence of CR with increasing concentrations from 0 to 20 μM. Meanwhile, we also successfully employed Mis-mPD-CDs to sense CR in bacteria including E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 5A). As the concentration of intracellular CR was increased from 0 to 60 μM, the green fluorescence of Mis-mPD-CDs was decreased gradually, showing that Mis-mPD-CDs can realize CR imaging and sensing in both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Collectively, Mis-mPD-CDs were harnessed to monitor CR in both eukaryotic cells and prokaryotic cells. Beside CLSM, Mis-mPD-CDs can also probe CR quantitatively in living cells via flow cytometry (Fig. 5B and C). As the amount of the intracellular CR in A549, C. albicans, E. coli, or S. aureus, was increased, the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs in these cells was reduced gradually, which corroborated well with the confocal imaging results (Fig. 5A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CR detection in real samples using Mis-mPD-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the successful sensing of intracellular CR using Mis-mPD-CDs in living cells, we would like to further examine whether the fluorescent Mis-mPD-CDs probe could be applied for CR detection in vivo. Here the zebrafish was chosen as a model organism for the research. Similar to the in vitro experiments, zebrafish were pretreated with 0, 5, 10, 15 or 20 μM CR for 4 h, and then incubated with CDs (100 μg/mL) for 1 h. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs decreased gradually in the zebrafish after the treatment of CR with the increasing concentrations. The above result demonstrates that Mis-mPD-CDs can be used for the in vivo semiquantitative detection of CR not only in living cells but also in living animals. Overall, Mis-mPD-CDs achieved satisfactory in vivo cellular imaging, as demonstrated in zebrafish.

In addition, Mis-mPD-CDs were further employed to determine CR quantitatively in fish tissues, and wastewater acquired from the paint industry. No CR was detected in either the fish tissues or the industrial wastewater (Table 1 ). Applying the standard addition strategy, the recoveries of CR were in the range from 94% to 109% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 6.6%, which was adequate for quantitative measurement in real samples. These findings demonstrate that the fluorescence measurement based on Mis-mPD-CDs has great potential for detecting CR in real samples.

To conclude, Mis-mPD-CDs were prepared from Miswak root and mPD with facile one-step hydrothermal reaction. Mis-mPD-CDs are negatively-charged, green emissive, and Ca, N, S-doped with high QY (19%). We found that the fluorescence intensity of Mis-mPD-CDs can be quenched by CR because of IFE. Thus, Mis-mPD-CDs was implemented for rapid CR assay with excellent sensitivity and high selectivity. The LOD is 58 nM, much lower than that of the existing CR assays [29–31]. More importantly, Mis-mPD-CDs can rapidly penetrate into and image a wide range of animal, fungal, and bacterial cells, achieving a fast universal cell imaging without sample washing. Also, Mis-mPD-CDs featured high stability and great biocompatibility. With these admirable properties, Mis-mPD-CDs were successfully deployed to monitor intracellular CR in living cells such as A549, C. albicans, E. coli, and S. aureus, and in vivo like zebra fish. Meanwhile, the quantitative detection of CR using Mis-mPD-CDs was achieved in real samples like fish tissues and industrial wastewater. Based on our knowledge, it is the first time to apply CDs for fluorescence detection of intracellular dyes, atoning the lack of in-situ intracellular CR assay. We envision that Mis-mPD-CDs will become an indispensable and powerful tool for sensitive measurement of CR in biological systems.
